request.question: Were Ivan Perestiani and Patricio Guzmán both directors?

title: Ivan Perestiani
Nostalgia for the Light
The Compass Rose (film)
Salvador Allende (film)
The Suram Fortress (film)
Patricio Guzmán
Pato Guzman
The Battle of Chile
The Pearl Button
Three Lives (film)
sentences: Ivan Nikolaevich Perestiani (also Ivane, Georgian: ივანე პერესტიანი ; Russian: Иван Николаевич Перестиани ; 13 April [O.S. 1 April] 1870 — 14 May 1959) was a Russian/Soviet film director, script-writer and actor, and People's Artist of the Georgian SSR (1949).
He was of Kefalonian Greek descent.
Nostalgia for the Light (Spanish: "Nostalgia de la Luz" ) is a documentary released in 2010 by Patricio Guzmán to address the lasting impacts of Augusto Pinochet’s dictatorship.

Guzmán focuses on the similarities between astronomers researching humanity’s past, in an astronomical sense, and the struggle of many Chilean women who still search, after decades, for the remnants of their relatives executed during the dictatorship.
Patricio Guzmán narrates the documentary himself and the documentary includes interviews and commentary from those affected and from astronomers and archeologists.
The Compass Rose (Spanish: "La rosa de los vientos" ) is a 1983 Spanish drama film directed by Patricio Guzmán.
It was entered into the 13th Moscow International Film Festival.
Salvador Allende is a 2004 documentary film about Chilean president Salvador Allende, from his election campaign to the "coup d'état" which ended his life.
It was directed by Patricio Guzmán and screened out of competition at the 2004 Cannes Film Festival.
The Suram Fortress (Georgian: სურამის ციხე , Russian: Сурамская крепость ) is a 1922 Soviet Georgian adventure film directed by Ivan Perestiani, based on Daniel Chonkadze's novel with the same title.
Patricio Guzmán Lozanes (born August 11, 1941) is a Chilean documentary film director.
He is internationally renowned for films such as "The Battle of Chile" and "Salvador Allende".
Patricio "Pato" Guzmán (1933–1991), a native of Chile, worked in the United States as an art director, production designer, and producer of television and film.
He worked on such notable shows as "The Jack Benny Program", "I Love Lucy", "The Lucy Show", "That Girl".

He also served as a production designer for the "" pilot "" (uncredited) and in that capacity worked directly with art director Matt Jeffries and creator-producer Gene Roddenberry on the design of the original USS Enterprise NCC-1701 exterior and bridge.
In the 1980s, Guzman worked with director Paul Mazursky on the films "Tempest", "Moscow on the Hudson", "Down and Out in Beverly Hills", and "".
The Battle of Chile is a documentary film directed by the Chilean Patricio Guzman, in three parts: "The Insurrection of the Bourgeoisie" (1975), "The Coup d'état" (1976), "Popular Power" (1979).
It is a chronicle of the political tension in Chile in 1973 and of the violent counter revolution against the democratically elected government of Salvador Allende.
It won the Grand Prix in 1975 and 1976 at the Grenoble International Film Festival.
In 1996, "Chile, Obstinate Memory" was released and followed Guzmán back to Chile as he screened the 3-part documentary to Chileans who had never seen it before.
The Pearl Button (Spanish: El botón de nácar ) is a 2015 Chilean documentary film directed by Patricio Guzmán.
It was screened in the main competition section of the 65th Berlin International Film Festival where it won the Silver Bear for Best Script.
It won the Lumières Award for Best Documentary at the 21st Lumières Awards.
The filmmaker has described the work as part of a triptych with "Nostalgia for the Light" and potentially a third film focusing on the Andes.
Three Lives (Georgian: სამი სიცოცხლე , Russian: Три жизни , sometimes "Who is to Blame?")
is a 1924 Georgian silent film directed by Ivan Perestiani.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Were Ivan Perestiani and Patricio Guzmán both directors?
hop1_docs: Ivan Perestiani | Ivan Nikolaevich Perestiani (also Ivane, Georgian: ივანე პერესტიანი ; Russian: Иван Николаевич Перестиани ; 13 April [O.S. 1 April] 1870 — 14 May 1959) was a Russian/Soviet film director, script-writer and actor, and People's Artist of the Georgian SSR (1949).  He was of Kefalonian Greek descent.

Patricio Guzmán | Patricio Guzmán Lozanes (born August 11, 1941) is a Chilean documentary film director.  He is internationally renowned for films such as "The Battle of Chile" and "Salvador Allende".

Nostalgia for the Light | Nostalgia for the Light (Spanish: "Nostalgia de la Luz" ) is a documentary released in 2010 by Patricio Guzmán to address the lasting impacts of Augusto Pinochet’s dictatorship.  Guzmán focuses on the similarities between astronomers researching humanity’s past, in an astronomical sense, and the struggle of many Chilean women who still search, after decades, for the remnants of their relatives executed during the dictatorship.  Patricio Guzmán narrates the documentary himself and the documentary includes interviews and commentary from those affected and from astronomers and archeologists.
In the Quagmire | In the Quagmire (Georgian: Gaplangva) is a 1927 Soviet silent film directed by Ivane Perestiani.
Nest of Wasps | Nest of Wasps (Georgian:Krazanas bude) is a 1927 Soviet silent film directed by Ivane Perestiani.
Ilan-dili | Ilan-dili is a 1926 Soviet silent adventure film directed by Ivane Perestiani.  It is the sequel to the film "The Savur Grave".

The Punishment of Shirvanskaya | The Punishment of Shirvanskaya (Georgian:Sasdjeli) is a 1926 Soviet silent adventure film directed by Ivane Perestiani.  It is a sequel to "The Crime of Shirvanskaya".
response.summary_1: Yes. Ivan Perestiani and Patricio Guzmán were both film directors.
response.hop2_query: "Ivan Perestiani film director biography" AND "Patricio Guzmán film director biography"
hop2_docs: Patricio Guzmán | Patricio Guzmán Lozanes (born August 11, 1941) is a Chilean documentary film director.  He is internationally renowned for films such as "The Battle of Chile" and "Salvador Allende".

Ivan Perestiani | Ivan Nikolaevich Perestiani (also Ivane, Georgian: ივანე პერესტიანი ; Russian: Иван Николаевич Перестиани ; 13 April [O.S. 1 April] 1870 — 14 May 1959) was a Russian/Soviet film director, script-writer and actor, and People's Artist of the Georgian SSR (1949).  He was of Kefalonian Greek descent.

Pato Guzman | Patricio "Pato" Guzmán (1933–1991), a native of Chile, worked in the United States as an art director, production designer, and producer of television and film.  He worked on such notable shows as "The Jack Benny Program", "I Love Lucy", "The Lucy Show", "That Girl".  He also served as a production designer for the "" pilot "" (uncredited) and in that capacity worked directly with art director Matt Jeffries and creator-producer Gene Roddenberry on the design of the original USS Enterprise NCC-1701 exterior and bridge.  In the 1980s, Guzman worked with director Paul Mazursky on the films "Tempest", "Moscow on the Hudson", "Down and Out in Beverly Hills", and "".

Peter Brunette | Peter Brunette (September 18, 1943 – June 16, 2010) was a film critic and film historian.  He was the author of several books, including biographies of Italian directors Roberto Rossellini and Michelangelo Antonioni.  Brunette’s last completed work was a book about Austrian director Michael Haneke, published in February 2010.

Marcel Lefebvre – Archbishop in Stormy Times | Monseigneur Lefebvre, un évêque dans la tempête (released as Marcel Lefebvre – Archbishop in Stormy Times in the US and UK) is a 2012 documentary film by French director Jacques-Régis du Cray, primarily based on the biography "A biography of Archbishop Lefebvre" written by Bishop Bernard Tissier de Mallerais.

Peter Stanford | Peter James Stanford (born 23 November 1961) is a British writer, editor, journalist and presenter, known for his biographies and writings on religion and ethics.  His biography of Lord Longford was the basis for the 2006 BAFTA-winning film "Longford" starring Jim Broadbent in the title role.  A former editor of the "Catholic Herald" newspaper, Stanford is also director of the Longford Trust for prison reform.

The Lost One | The Lost One (German: Der Verlorene) is a 1951 West German drama film directed by Peter Lorre and starring Lorre, Karl John and Renate Mannhardt.  It is an art film in the film noir style, based on a true story.  Lorre wrote, directed, and starred in this film, his only film as director or writer.  The film's translated name has been used as the title of his biography.
hop2_titles: Marcel Lefebvre – Archbishop in Stormy Times
response.summary_2: Yes. Ivan Perestiani was a Russian/Soviet film director, and Patricio Guzmán is a Chilean documentary film director.
